1:
The chapter opens upon an ominous calm, and the witnesses reckon its measure the same way. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott, with the reformed John Gill and the Wesleyan Adam Clarke, all date the three years without war from the covenant Ahab made with Ben-hadad in 1 Kings 20:34 — the treaty by which the beaten Syrian was released on a bare promise to restore the captured cities. Ellicott observes that Syria has since recovered its independence, has not surrendered Ramoth-gilead, and is now strong enough to face the united armies of Israel and Judah, so that the sagacity of the prophetic rebuke in 1 Kings 20:42 has been amply justified. Gill and Clarke add that the very quiet was a reprieve: Elijah had threatened that Ahab's life should go for Ben-hadad's, and (Gill notes, following the older rabbis) the sentence was respited three years because Ahab had humbled himself in sackcloth.

The reformed and puritan voices read the broken promise morally. The Puritan Matthew Henry makes Ben-hadad the very type of the faithless ally: Ahab had 'foolishly took his word' instead of holding him until the cities were actually restored, and now learns by hard experience that 'as the kisses, so the promises, of an enemy are deceitful,' and that no confidence is to be placed in leagues extorted by distress. Ben-hadad, he says, is one of those princes who think themselves bound by their word no longer than it serves their interest. So Ramoth-gilead — no ordinary town, but a Levites' city and one of the cities of refuge — remained in Syrian hands, and Ahab resolves to prove that the hand which gave Ben-hadad peace can give him trouble.

Henry presses a sharper paradox still. Ahab 'has a good cause, yet succeeds not,' from which he draws that 'equity is not to be judged of by prosperity' — a war may be just and its outcome still be ruin, when God has an older reckoning to settle. Even the three years' peace, Henry suggests, was a mercy granted as a reward for Ahab's outward professions of repentance, a lengthening of tranquillity to a man already under the sentence of 1 Kings 21:19. The calm is real, but it is the stillness before the storm the prophet had foreseen.
2:
The witnesses turn from the truce to the visit that undoes it, and they read Jehoshaphat's descent to Samaria as an alliance more dangerous than it looked. Keil and Delitzsch supply the background the narrative assumes: the two houses had already been joined by marriage, Jehoshaphat's son taking Ahab's daughter (2 Chronicles 18:1; 2 Kings 8:18), so that the king of Judah came down to Israel as kinsman and ally. The reformed John Gill marks the timing precisely — it was in the third year of the peace, before it had run its course, that Jehoshaphat came.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh what the alliance cost. Charles Ellicott draws on the fuller record of the Chronicler: the early part of Jehoshaphat's reign had seen the prosperity of Asa continued and increased, yet the same account adds, 'in significant connection,' that he 'had riches and honour in abundance, and joined affinity with Ahab' (2 Chronicles 18:1). Ellicott reads this to mean that Judah's very prosperity had become, at least in part, dependent on a change of policy from enmity to alliance — a dependence 'dangerous alike spiritually and politically,' though probably thought a necessity. The visit itself, he notes from 2 Chronicles 18:2, was one of festivity, of which Ahab shrewdly took advantage.

Keil and Delitzsch let the reader hear the moment when hospitality tips into war. Ahab tells his court that the Syrian has kept Ramoth in Gilead, which by the terms of 1 Kings 20:34 he ought to have surrendered, and then asks his guest whether he will go up with him. The pledge is total — 'my people as thy people, my horses as thy horses' — which the Chronicler compresses to 'I am with thee in the war' (compare 2 Chronicles 18:3). Gill's small point about the timing gains weight beside this: the war is conceived before the treaty of peace has even lapsed, so that Ahab breaks the calm he had been granted rather than wait out its term. The voices do not quarrel over the facts; together they let the feast disclose its hook, and a good king is drawn, by kinship and courtesy, toward a battle that will nearly cost him his life. What begins as a family reunion and a royal banquet ends as the muster for the field of Ramoth, and the reader is left to weigh how easily good company can carry a good man where he ought not to go.
3:
The verse is Ahab's opening move, and the witnesses attend to the city he covets and the men he will consult. John Gill notes the tense the older versions blur — Ahab 'had said' this to his court, his privy councillors, some little time before Jehoshaphat arrived; the design was already formed. Charles Ellicott traces the pedigree of the disputed town: Ramoth in Gilead is first named as a city of refuge in the territory of Gad (Deuteronomy 4:43; Joshua 20:8; Joshua 21:38), then as the centre of one of Solomon's provinces with its strong hill country of Argob (1 Kings 4:13); in the Syrian wars it becomes a frontier fortress, taken and retaken. It had fallen to the Syrians and been withheld against promise, and Ellicott looks ahead to its recovery, when Ahab's defeat and death would be avenged by Jehoram, who took the city and held it (2 Kings 9:1-14).

The most searching comment belongs to Keil and Delitzsch, who fix the ambiguous religious status of the four hundred prophets Ahab will summon. They were not, K&D argue, the prophets of Asherah who had failed to appear at Carmel (1 Kings 18:19-20), nor prophets of Baal, for Ahab proposes to inquire of them 'the word of Jehovah,' which he could not have done of Baal's men; yet neither were they true prophets of the Lord and disciples of the schools. They are something in between — court prophets of the calf-worship, wearing Jehovah's name over a corrupted service. That single distinction, quietly made here, is the seed of the whole drama to follow.

Between the reformed and grammatical-historical readings there is no dispute of substance, only a division of labour: Gill fixes the moment, Ellicott the place, Keil and Delitzsch the men. Together they set the stage. Ahab has decided; the ground is a hallowed city long lost; and the counsel he will seek comes from a body of prophets who can pronounce the sacred name without speaking for the God who owns it. The reader is prepared to watch a king ask heaven a question he has already answered for himself.
4:
Jehoshaphat's answer — 'I am as thou art, my people as thy people, my horses as thy horses' — is the hinge on which the witnesses hang their warning, and they read it as generosity that has forgotten its bounds. The reformed John Gill takes Ahab's request first: his own forces were small, and so able an auxiliary as Judah would be of great advantage. Charles Ellicott hears in Jehoshaphat's reply a note of deference as well as friendship toward the stronger kingdom, and pauses to remember that, whatever else is true of him, Ahab had by now returned to the outward worship of the Lord.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke names the root of the entanglement. The good understanding between the two kings, he says, was the fruit of the matrimonial alliance between Jehoshaphat's son Jehoram and Ahab's daughter Athaliah (2 Chronicles 18:1; 2 Kings 8:18); and this very coalition did not please God, for which Jehoshaphat was afterward severely reproved by Jehu the seer (2 Chronicles 19:1-3). The unequal yoke that looks like statecraft is, to Clarke, a sin already contracted.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the pastoral lesson without excusing the fault. He grants that even bad men have often coveted the friendship of the good, and that it is desirable 'to have those with us that have God with them'; what he cannot commend is that Jehoshaphat should go so entirely into Ahab's interest as to say 'I am as thou art.' Too great a complaisance toward evildoers, Henry warns, has brought many good people, through unwariness, into dangerous fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness — and Jehoshaphat 'had like to have paid dearly for his compliment' when, in the battle, he was taken for Ahab. Yet Henry notes the one redeeming thread: in joining Israel against Syria the son atoned, after a fashion, for his father Asa's fault in hiring Syria against Israel (1 Kings 15:19-20). The witnesses land together on a single caution: piety does not sanctify an alliance that piety would never have chosen, and the warmth of Jehoshaphat's answer is precisely what makes it perilous — an unqualified 'I am as thou art' leaves no room to draw back when the venture turns to ruin.
5:
Amid the rush toward war Jehoshaphat interposes a single sentence of piety — 'Inquire, I pray thee, at the word of the LORD to day' — and the witnesses fasten on it as the one wholesome instinct in the scene. The reformed John Gill reads it straight from the man's character: being a pious and religious prince, Jehoshaphat would not go into a war at once, without first consulting the Lord by his prophets whether it was his will that they should engage and prosper; and he wanted it done immediately, before matters were carried any further. The little word 'to day' is not incidental — it presses for counsel before the momentum of the alliance becomes irreversible.

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the request a portrait of the godly man abroad. Whithersoever a good man goes, he says, he desires to take God along with him, and will acknowledge him in all his ways, asking leave of him and looking up to him for success. And whithersoever a good man goes, he ought to take his religion with him, and not be ashamed to own it — 'no, not when he is with those who have no kindness for it.' Jehoshaphat had not left behind him at Jerusalem his affection and reverence for the word of the Lord, but both avowed it and endeavoured to introduce it into Ahab's court: if Ahab drew him into his wars, he would draw Ahab into his devotions.

There is no debate among the witnesses here, and the verse's own irony is what they leave the reader to feel. Jehoshaphat asks for 'the word of the LORD' in the presence of a king who keeps four hundred prophets precisely so that he need never hear it. The question is exactly right, and it is asked in exactly the wrong company; yet its rightness is not thereby cancelled. It will summon Micaiah, and through Micaiah the true word — which is the whole reason the chapter turns from a war council into a trial of prophecy. One honest instinct, spoken aloud among flatterers, is enough to change everything that follows.
6:
Ahab's response to the pious request is to convene a chorus, and the witnesses agree that the four hundred are a counterfeit. John Gill calls them false prophets, Ahab's own and not the Lord's — probably those prophets of the groves who ate at Jezebel's table and were spared when the prophets of Baal were destroyed, since the number answers to them. Adam Clarke likewise reckons them the prophets of Asherah maintained by Jezebel, absent from the contest on Carmel (1 Kings 18:19). Charles Ellicott refines the point: they were clearly not avowed prophets of Baal or the Asherah, as their words in v.12 show, yet Jehoshaphat's discontent proves they were not true prophets of Jehovah either; most likely they were devoted, like the old prophet of Bethel, to the idolatry of Jeroboam.

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the unanimous verdict itself — 'Go up; for the Lord shall deliver it into the hand of the king.' Ahab put the question with a show of fairness, 'Shall I go, or shall I forbear?', but the answer was foreordained by the flattery it was hired to give. K&D, with Gill and Ellicott, note that these men borrow the name of Jehovah while serving another master; the Antiochene comparison writes itself, for the scene has the ring of Homer's Calchas, the seer who never told Agamemnon a pleasant truth turned inside out.

The Puritan Matthew Henry exposes the machinery of the deception. To please Jehoshaphat the prophets made use of the name Jehovah, stealing the word from the true prophets (compare Jeremiah 23:30); to please Ahab they said 'Go up.' They had probabilities on their side — Ahab had lately beaten the Syrians twice, held a good cause, and was strengthened by Judah's alliance — but they pretended to speak by divine foresight, not human conjecture, dressing a shrewd political guess in the borrowed authority of revelation. Henry's warning is that unanimity is no proof of truth: 'here were four hundred men that prophesied with one mind and one mouth, and yet all in an error.' Those who love to be flattered, he adds, shall never want for flatterers, and it is a just judgment of God to give up those to their own counsels who give themselves up to their own lusts.
7:
Jehoshaphat is not satisfied, and the witnesses read his careful question — 'Is there not here a prophet of the LORD besides?' — as the discernment of a man who can feel a false note without being able to name it. The reformed John Gill puts it delicately: Jehoshaphat suspected these to be false prophets, though he would not call them so, nor suggest to Ahab's face that they were not the Lord's prophets, lest he give offence to a king who thought well of them; so he asks whether there is any other 'that went under the character of a prophet of the Lord,' for his further satisfaction.

The most illuminating comment is Charles Ellicott's, and it turns on a single divine name that the English 'the Lord' conceals. In the prophets' own utterance, Ellicott observes, the word was Adonai, 'Lord' in the general sense, which might name the supreme god of any religion; but Jehoshaphat, 'struck with their shrinking from the distinctive name Jehovah,' asks for a prophet of Jehovah — one who is not ashamed or afraid to speak in that awful and covenant name. The counterfeits had given themselves away not by what they claimed but by the name they would not use.

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the discernment a matter of spiritual sense. The false prophets could not so mimic the true but that one whose senses were exercised could detect the fallacy; Jehoshaphat 'was too much of a courtier' to reflect openly on the king's chaplains, yet he waits for a prophet of the Lord, intimating plainly that he could not look upon these as such. Borrowing the apostle's phrase, Henry says that whatever these prophets seemed to be, 'in conference they added nothing to him' (compare Galatians 2:6) — one faithful prophet of the Lord was worth them all. The witnesses are of one mind: true religion has an ear that mere numbers and borrowed names cannot deceive. And there is a courtesy in Jehoshaphat's method worth marking, for he neither denounces the king's chaplains nor pretends to be content with them; he simply asks for something more, and in the asking exposes the lack. It is the quiet persistence of a conscience that will not be talked out of what it knows, and it is the hinge on which the whole scene now turns toward the one prophet Ahab dreads.
8:
Ahab's reluctant admission — 'There is yet one man... but I hate him' — draws from the witnesses a study in a conscience that knows the truth and resents it. The reformed John Gill notes the loneliness of the true prophet: but one such man in Samaria, for Elijah and Elisha were elsewhere. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott identify him with the unnamed prophet who had rebuked Ahab for releasing Ben-hadad and threatened him with judgment (1 Kings 20:35), which, on the testimony of Josephus, explains both Ahab's grudge and Micaiah's imprisonment. Ellicott adds that the very name Micaiah, 'who is like Jehovah,' stands as a silent reproach among prophets who would not speak that name.

The heart of the matter is Ahab's stated reason for his hatred: 'he doth not prophesy good concerning me, but evil.' The Puritan Matthew Henry turns this back on the king with unsparing logic. Whose fault is it that Micaiah brings only evil tidings? 'If Ahab had done well, he would have heard nothing but good from heaven; if he do ill, he may thank himself for all the uneasiness which the reproofs and threats of God's word gave him.' Those are wretchedly hardened in sin, Henry says, and ripening apace for ruin, who hate God's ministers for dealing plainly with them, and who 'reckon those their enemies that tell them the truth.'

Jehoshaphat's mild remonstrance — 'Let not the king say so' — the witnesses take as too gentle. Henry judges that Jehoshaphat should have told Ahab plainly that in speaking thus he was unjust to the prophet, unkind to himself, and affronted the prophet's Lord and his own; 'such sinners as Ahab must be rebuked sharply.' Yet the reproof, soft as it was, prevailed so far that Ahab, for fear of provoking his ally, consented to send. The witnesses agree on the diagnosis: Ahab does not doubt Micaiah's truth — he grants it by hating it — and that is the surest mark of a soul at war with the word that would save it. It is a strange testimony the king pays the prophet in his very complaint, for by owning that Micaiah's word comes true he confesses him a prophet of the Lord even while he resents the office; the enmity is itself an unwilling witness to the truth it means to silence.
9:
The brief note of the summons — Ahab 'called an officer' to fetch Micaiah quickly — gives the witnesses occasion for one small but telling observation about the man sent. John Gill, Adam Clarke, and Keil and Delitzsch agree that the word rendered 'officer' is saris, literally a eunuch or chamberlain of the court (compare 1 Samuel 8:15; Genesis 37:36). Clarke adds the reason such a servant was probably a foreigner: it was not lawful to reduce an Israelite to that condition, so the demeaning office fell to a stranger. K&D catch the urgency in the command 'bring quickly' — Ahab, having yielded to Jehoshaphat, will not let the matter cool.

Beneath the administrative detail the witnesses see the shape of Ahab's whole posture. He knows exactly where to find Micaiah, which confirms, as the Puritan Matthew Henry had inferred, that the prophet was already in custody — a bound man whose imprisonment had not silenced the word of the Lord in his mouth. The king who keeps four hundred prophets on call must send a chamberlain to the prison to hear the one voice he cannot control. That contrast is the verse's quiet irony: royal machinery bends, at a foreign king's request, to summon the very prisoner whose message Ahab most dreads.

There is no dispute among so few and so agreed a company. The reformed and grammatical-historical voices simply fix the philology, and the Puritan draws the lesson: God's word is not bound though its messenger is. The whole apparatus of the court — thrones, robes, chamberlains, a chorus of prophets — is now marshalled to receive a single captive, and the reader is made to feel that all this pomp is about to be measured against one man who has nothing but the truth. The stage is set for the confrontation the chapter has been building toward. Gill's philological note and Henry's inference meet in a single irony: the smallest functionary of the palace is dispatched in haste to fetch the one man whose word the whole assembly least wishes to hear, and the speed of the errand only quickens the reckoning that all this majesty is powerless to avert.
10:
The scene assembles in state, and the witnesses read the royal display as a stage deliberately set to overawe the coming prisoner. John Gill notes the two kings seated each on his throne 'in great state and majesty,' robed, in an open place at the entrance of the gate of Samaria. Keil and Delitzsch render the setting exactly: a goren, a threshing-floor, that is, a levelled place in the open air before the gate, where the kings sat in royal attire while the prophets of the calves went on prophesying success. Charles Ellicott reads the arrangement as calculated: having reluctantly consented to send for Micaiah, Ahab 'seeks to overawe him by display not only of royal pomp, but of prophetic inspiration,' the whole chorus professing to speak, like Micaiah, from Jehovah.

Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the most theatrical of the prophets, whose sign-act belongs as much to v.11 but sets the tone here. Zedekiah the son of Chenaanah made himself horns of iron and cried, 'With these shalt thou thrust down Aram until it be destroyed' — an embodiment, K&D observe, of the very figure Moses had used in blessing Joseph, 'his horns are the horns of the wild ox, with them shall he push the peoples' (Deuteronomy 33:17). The false prophet borrows a true blessing and misapplies it, transferring to Ahab a promise that belonged to Ephraim.

The witnesses do not contend; they combine into a single tableau of counterfeit glory. Two crowned kings, a threshing-floor turned throne-room, four hundred voices in chorus, and a prophet crowned with iron horns — all of it arranged to make the truth, when it comes, look small and solitary. The irony the commentators leave hanging is that the pomp is a confession of fear: a king sure of his cause would not need so vast a machinery of reassurance. The greater the show of certainty, the nearer, one senses, the collapse it is meant to hide. Ellicott's word 'overawe' is the key to the tableau, for everything is calculated to make the coming prisoner feel the weight of majesty and multitude arrayed against him; and yet, as the sequel proves, all this staging serves only to give the truth a grander platform from which to be vindicated.
11:
Zedekiah's horns of iron give the witnesses their study of counterfeit prophecy dressed in the forms of the true. John Gill explains the emblem: horns are tokens of power and might, and iron ones of strength greater still; and Gill grants that the true prophets themselves sometimes used such visible signs, so that Zedekiah is aping a genuine practice. Adam Clarke says the same — this was in imitation of that kind of prophecy 'which instructed by significative actions,' frequent among the Lord's own prophets. The forgery is clever precisely because the form is legitimate.

Charles Ellicott presses the point through the name and the pedigree of the symbol. Zedekiah — 'righteousness of Jehovah' — bears a name that professes devotion to the true God, and it is he who first here utters that Name; symbolic action, Ellicott notes, was not infrequent in the prophets (compare 1 Kings 11:30), and the horn as an emblem of victorious strength is thoroughly biblical, from the oracle of Balaam (Numbers 23:22) and the blessing of Moses (Deuteronomy 33:17) to the song of Hannah (1 Samuel 2:1) and the visions of Daniel and Zechariah (Daniel 8:3; Zechariah 1:18-19). Everything about the act is orthodox except its truth.

The witnesses converge on the lesson the whole chapter presses: the closest imitation of prophecy is the most dangerous. Keil and Delitzsch tie Zedekiah's 'Thus saith the LORD' to the false transfer of a promise not his to give. The Puritan Matthew Henry, looking back on the scene, will note the grim reversal — that Zedekiah's iron horns would in the end push, not the Syrians, but Ahab himself, into destruction. Here the emblem is all confidence; the reader, warned, watches a man wield the language and gestures of the Spirit while a lying spirit speaks through him. It is the peculiar danger of the counterfeit that it can copy every outward mark of the genuine — the name, the sign, the confident 'Thus saith the LORD' — and lack only the one thing that cannot be copied, the truth of God behind the words. Zedekiah's horns gleam brightest at the very moment they are most hollow.
12:
The chorus reaches its crescendo — 'Go up to Ramoth-gilead, and prosper' — and the witnesses hear in the swelling unanimity a warning rather than a comfort. John Gill notes simply that all the prophets prophesied as Zedekiah had, encouraging the king and promising victory. Charles Ellicott makes the sharper observation: the prophets, led by Zedekiah, 'now venture to use the Name of Jehovah, from which they had at first shrunk.' Their growing boldness with the sacred name keeps step with their growing excitement, and Ellicott finds the whole picture uncomfortably familiar.

For Ellicott the reiterated cry recalls another frenzied chorus. The description of their united, mounting clamour 'reminds us of the repeated "O Baal, hear us" of Mount Carmel,' and stands in the same stark contrast with the calm, stern utterance of the true prophet that Elijah's silence had once thrown into relief. The volume and the vehemence are the point: false prophecy persuades by numbers and heat, where true prophecy needs neither. The four hundred are loudest exactly where they are most wrong.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, surveying the same moment, draws the conclusion the reader is meant to carry away: 'Unity is not always the mark of a true church and a true ministry.' Here were four hundred men prophesying with one mind and one mouth, and yet all in an error. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that a consensus purchased by flattery and pronounced in the borrowed name of Jehovah is no safer than a single lie — indeed it is more dangerous, because it wears the whole appearance of heaven's agreement while heaven itself has decreed the opposite, as the next scene will disclose. There is, too, a dramatic irony the reader cannot miss: knowing already how the campaign ends, we hear the confident cry 'and prosper' as the very word that seals Ahab's doom, since it is by trusting exactly this chorus that he goes up to die. The louder the promise of success, the surer the coming defeat, and the four hundred are never so wrong as when they are most agreed. Their word will be answered — but only by the lone prophet whose entrance the next verses bring, and whose single sentence will outweigh the whole assembly.
13:
The officer's whispered counsel on the road — 'let thy word be like the word of one of them, and speak that which is good' — becomes for the witnesses a revealing glimpse into how the worldly imagine prophecy works. John Gill sets the scene from Josephus: the messenger spoke to Micaiah by the way, as they came along together. Adam Clarke fastens on the assumption buried in the request: 'What notion could these men have of prophecy, when they supposed it was in the power of the prophet to model the prediction as he pleased, and have the result accordingly?' The officer thinks a prophecy is a policy, adjustable to please the king.

Charles Ellicott develops this into a meditation on a perennial religious confusion. There runs through the whole history, he says, that 'strange confusion of idea, so common in superstition at all times,' which in one breath believes the prophets are inspired by God and in the next fancies their inspiration can be directed at will — bribed, beguiled, or coerced to 'prophesy smooth things.' Its extreme form is Simon Magus, who believed the apostles truly conferred the Spirit and yet madly supposed the power could be bought (Acts 8:18-19); its milder form is the mingled awe and contempt Balak felt for Balaam. Such delusion, Ellicott says, is silenced only by declarations like Micaiah's, or Balaam's own (Numbers 22:18), and is never truly rooted out except by real spiritual knowledge of God.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the officer's motive with a shrewd eye. He thought Micaiah 'altogether such a one as the rest, who studied to please men and not God,' and hinted that compliance might purchase not only release but preferment; for 'those that dote upon worldly things themselves think every body else should do so too.' The witnesses agree: the tempter offers the prophet the world's whole philosophy in a sentence, and the prophet's fidelity in the next verse is the refutation of it. Truth is not a commodity to be tuned to the market, and the man of God cannot sell what is not his to alter.
14:
Micaiah's oath — 'As the LORD liveth, what the LORD saith unto me, that will I speak' — the witnesses receive as the plain answer to the officer's temptation and the standard by which all true ministry is measured. John Gill notes that the prophet swore by the living God for the confirmation of what he was about to say, staking the most solemn form of speech on the fidelity of his message. Keil and Delitzsch underline that Micaiah replied to the road-side pressure 'with a solemn oath, that he would only speak what Jehovah said to him' — neither more nor less, and nothing altered to suit the court.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the resolution its full dignity. Being pressed, Micaiah 'protests, and backs his protestation with an oath, that he will deliver his message from God with all faithfulness, whether it be pleasing or displeasing to his prince': what the Lord saith, 'that will I speak, without addition, diminution, or alteration.' This was nobly resolved, Henry says, and became one who had his eye on a greater King than either of these — a King arrayed in brighter robes and seated on a higher throne than the two who waited in the gate of Samaria.

The witnesses stand as one, and the verse yields the chapter's positive definition of a prophet over against the four hundred. Where the hired chorus tuned its message to the hearer, Micaiah binds himself by oath to a message he has not chosen and cannot change. The contrast is total: their liberty to please is really bondage to men; his bondage to the word is really freedom before God. Everything the reader has watched — the pomp, the horns, the mounting cry of v.12 — is now set against one man's simple determination to say only what is true, and the trial of the two prophecies can at last begin. Henry's application reaches past the gate of Samaria to every pulpit since: the minister who tunes his message to his hearers has already sold the office, while the one who binds himself to speak God's word whole, welcome or unwelcome, keeps faith with the only Master whose approval finally matters. Micaiah's oath is the charter of that fidelity.
15:
Micaiah's opening words repeat the flatterers' oracle verbatim — 'Go, and prosper; for the LORD shall deliver it into the hand of the king' — and the witnesses agree he means the exact opposite of what he says. The Puritan Matthew Henry reads it as studied irony: Ahab had put to him the same question he had put to his own prophets, 'like Balaam... strongly bent to do his own' will, and Micaiah gave him back his own prophets' words 'with such an air and pronunciation as plainly showed he spoke it by way of derision.' The sense, Henry paraphrases, is: 'I know you are determined to go, and your prophets are unanimous; go then, and take what follows.' Those deserve to be bantered, he adds, who love to be flattered. Charles Ellicott hears the same tone and traces its lineage — Micaiah is 'a true disciple of Elijah in the defiant irony' with which he mocks the false prophets so bitterly as at once to show Ahab his scorn of them and of him.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke adds the chapter's most learned note, fastening on the studied ambiguity of the oracle itself. The word it is not in the Hebrew: 'the LORD shall deliver into the king's hand' can mean Ramoth into Ahab's hand, or Israel into the hand of the king of Syria — so that, however the battle fell, the juggling prophet could claim he had foretold the event. Clarke likens it to the double-tongued oracles of the heathen, by which the priests preserved their credit whatever happened: the Delphic answer to Croesus that a great empire would fall, or the famous line given to Pyrrhus, 'Aio te, Aeacida, Romanos vincere posse' — 'I say that thou, son of Aeacus, the Romans canst conquer,' which reads with equal ease 'that thou the Romans can conquer thee.' Micaiah throws the ambiguous words back in mockery, exposing their fallacy by echoing them.

Ahab, stung, adjures him to speak nothing but truth in the name of the Lord, which the witnesses take as proof that the irony struck home. Henry notes the pretence in it — Ahab affects a sincere wish to know God's mind, yet means to paint the prophet as a sullen, ill-humoured man who will not tell the truth until forced. The voices land together on the paradox of the moment: the one honest speaker in the assembly is heard, at first, saying exactly what the liars said, and only the tone betrays the difference. It is a parable of how the world receives the prophet — willing enough to hear him while he seems to agree, indignant the instant it perceives he does not.
16:
The king's exasperated demand — 'How many times shall I adjure thee that thou tell me nothing but that which is true in the name of the LORD?' — the witnesses read as the moment Ahab's irony-detector betrays his own bad conscience. The reformed John Gill handles the odd arithmetic: not that Ahab had adjured him repeatedly before, but that he asks how often he must do it, and now adjures him 'once for all,' that he might not be forced to repeat it. The phrase reveals a man who wants the truth and dreads it in the same breath.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the psychology the narrative leaves implicit. From the very agreement of Micaiah's first reply with the flatterers' — and, they add, 'probably also from the tone in which he spoke' — Ahab perceived that the words were ironical, and so adjured him to speak only what was true in Jehovah's name. It is a striking admission: the king can tell the true prophet's mockery from the false prophets' sincerity, which means he can tell truth from falsehood well enough when it suits him, and is therefore without excuse. The very demand for a true word is a confession that he knows the chorus of four hundred has given him none.

The witnesses do not differ; they let the verse expose the self-contradiction at the heart of Ahab's religion. He keeps four hundred prophets precisely so as never to be told the truth, and then, unsatisfied, solemnly charges the one man he has imprisoned to tell him nothing but the truth in the name of the God he has spent his reign defying. The reader feels the futility of it: Ahab will get his true word now, and having got it will refuse it. To ask for truth while resolved not to obey it is the oldest form of self-deception, and the adjuration of v.16 is its portrait — a hunger for certainty joined to a fixed purpose to disregard whatever certainty brings. Gill's small point about the number of adjurations only deepens the irony: Ahab makes a solemn ceremony of demanding one true word from the very prophet he means to keep in chains, as though the form of an oath could compel a message he has already resolved to ignore. The machinery of sincerity is all in place, and the will to obey is entirely absent.
17:
Adjured to speak plainly, Micaiah gives his first vision — 'I saw all Israel scattered upon the hills, as sheep that have not a shepherd' — and the witnesses read it as the whole disaster foretold in a single tender image. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads it directly: here the prophet foretells the defeat of Israel and the death of the king; they are as sheep without a shepherd, a people without a master, because the political shepherd and master, Ahab, shall fall in battle. The scattering is the army's; the shepherd's fall is the king's.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the pathos and the mercy folded into the picture. Micaiah saw the host dispersed on the mountains, 'as sheep that had no one to guide them' — smite the shepherd, and the sheep are scattered (compare Zechariah 13:7). Yet Henry marks the Lord's own gentle word over them, 'These have no master; let them return every man to his house in peace.' The vision does not foresee a great slaughter of the people but a dishonourable retreat: Israel is bereaved of its king, obliged to withdraw without accomplishing its object, yet the common soldiers are sent home in peace, no great losers by the death of the one man who was marked for ruin.

The reformed and grammatical-historical readings run the same way, and the witnesses converge on the verse's austere precision. Micaiah does not rage or curse; he simply reports what he has seen, testifying, as Henry says, to what he had seen and heard while the others prophesied out of their own hearts (compare Jeremiah 23:28). The image is the more devastating for its calm: a whole kingdom's war reduced to a flock without a shepherd, and its proud, defiant king reduced to the single sheep whose fall breaks the fold. The truth, spoken without heat, needs no ornament; the picture itself is the sentence. And the mercy Henry finds folded into it is worth marking, for even in pronouncing the king's doom the vision spares the people: the flock is scattered but not slaughtered, sent home rather than destroyed, so that the judgment falls where the guilt lies and stops there. It is a portrait of a God whose sentences are exact, wounding the one who provoked them and releasing the many who followed him blindly to the field.
18:
Ahab turns from the prophet to his guest with a told-you-so — 'Did I not tell thee that he would prophesy no good concerning me, but evil?' — and the witnesses read it as the reflex of a guilty mind. The reformed John Gill notes plainly what Ahab has perceived: the prophet has foretold that he should fall in battle, and the king hears in it only personal spite. Keil and Delitzsch sharpen the point — though Ahab had himself asked for a true word of the Lord, he now tries to attribute the unfavourable prophecy to Micaiah's private enmity, as if the message were nothing more than the man's dislike dressed as revelation.

The Puritan Matthew Henry exposes the projection at work. 'Those that bear malice to others,' he observes, 'are generally willing to believe that others bear malice to them, though they have no cause for it,' and are therefore quick 'to put the worst constructions upon all they say.' Ahab reads his own heart into the prophet's. And Henry answers the charge with a plain question: what evil did Micaiah prophesy to Ahab in warning him that this expedition would be fatal, while he was still free to choose whether to proceed? 'The greatest kindness we can do to one that is going a dangerous way is to tell him of his danger.'

The witnesses stand together, and the verse becomes a small anatomy of how truth is received by those who will not have it. Ahab does not dispute that Micaiah's word is true; he complains only that it is unwelcome, and treats the messenger's fidelity as a grudge. It is the perennial error of the hardened heart, to hear a warning as an insult and a physician as an enemy. The prophet who most loves the king — for love it is, to risk the dungeon by telling him the truth — is precisely the one the king most resents, while the flatterers who are leading him to his death are embraced as friends. So the good word is charged with the malice that belongs entirely to the refusal of it.
19:
Micaiah now lifts the veil on the false prophecy itself, and the witnesses agree that his vision of the heavenly court — 'I saw the LORD sitting on his throne, and all the host of heaven standing by him on his right hand and on his left' — is to be read as a divinely-given parable, not a literal transcript of events in heaven. The reformed John Gill insists that all of it is 'represented as in a vision, in which things are brought down to the capacities of men, and not as really transacted': Micaiah discloses the hidden ground of the false prophecy, that the Lord had suffered a lying spirit to work Ahab's ruin. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the prophet lays bare 'the hidden ground of the false prophecy of his four hundred prophets.'

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke makes the parabolic reading explicit and pastoral. This, he says, 'is a mere parable, and only tells in figurative language what was in the womb of providence' — the events soon to take place, the agents employed, and God's permission for them to act. Micaiah did not choose to say bluntly before the angry king, 'Thy prophets are all liars, and the devil, the father of lies, dwells in them'; instead he clothes the same truth in a vision, saying it 'in language as forcible, but less offensive.' The form is mercy as well as art.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the doctrine the vision was framed to teach. 'You have your thrones,' Micaiah in effect tells the two kings, 'and think you may do what you will; but I saw the Lord sitting upon his throne' — a King above all kings, whose throne is above all the thrones of earthly princes, who 'is not a man, as you are.' Henry marks the innumerable company of angels who stand ready to go where he sends them, and the truth that God 'not only takes cognizance of, but presides over, all the affairs of this lower world.' The witnesses do not divide; they read the scene as the Bible's own way of showing that the rise and fall of kings is governed from a throne no earthly pomp can rival, and that the battle at Ramoth was decided in heaven before it was ever joined on earth.
20:
The Lord's question in the vision — 'Who shall persuade Ahab, that he may go up and fall at Ramoth-gilead?' — the witnesses guard carefully against a crude literalism, while pressing the certainty of the decree it dramatizes. The reformed John Gill is explicit: 'not that it can be supposed that the Lord entered into a consultation with the angels upon this subject'; the scene expresses in the form of a deliberation what is in truth the settled decree of God, that Ahab should go up to Ramoth and fall there by the hand of the very man he had let go — 'a just punishment of him.' The question is a picture, not a perplexity in the mind of God.

Keil and Delitzsch let the vision unfold in its own idiom: 'one said on this manner, and another said on that manner,' until a spirit came forth to volunteer. K&D keep the reader's eye on the shape of the narrative — a heavenly council, a proposal invited, an offer made — as the divinely-chosen way to reveal that the flattering unanimity of the four hundred is no accident but the appointed instrument of judgment. What looks from below like the free counsel of Ahab's prophets is, seen from above, the executing of a sentence already passed.

The witnesses land together on the moral core of the picture. The word fall is the hinge: the whole deliberation is ordered toward Ahab's fall at Ramoth, which is not the arbitrary cruelty of heaven but the ripening of his own long defiance — the sparing of Ben-hadad, the blood of Naboth, the altars of Baal. God's providence does not create Ahab's wickedness; it overrules the wickedness that is already there, so that the king's own chosen counsellors become the means of his destruction. The vision thus answers the deepest question the chapter raises — how a man surrounded by prophets can walk knowingly to his death — by showing that a judicial hardening is at work, and that the God who is mocked is not thereby defeated but is, even in the lie, accomplishing his righteous end.
21:
The verse where 'there came forth a spirit' to volunteer for the deception opens the one genuine interpretive division of the passage: what exactly is this spirit? The reformed John Gill answers without hesitation — it is not one of the pure and holy host who stand on the right hand and the left, 'for they are... impeccable, and cannot lie or deceive,' but the evil spirit, Satan himself, 'the father of lies, the old deceiver, who came forth from his own place and his own company.' On Gill's reading the volunteer is the devil, present even in the courts of heaven as in the opening of Job, offering himself as the agent of a judgment God has decreed.

Charles Ellicott hesitates before that identification and canvasses the alternatives raised by the definite article, 'the spirit.' Some, he says rather weakly, take it as merely anticipatory of the description that follows; others as 'the spirit of prophecy,' an emanation from the Godhead conceived as the medium of inspiration — 'an expression conceivable, but certainly unprecedented.' Ellicott's own preference is to avoid importing the full later idea of 'the Satan' (as in Job 1; 1 Chronicles 21:1; Zechariah 3:1-2) and to read it as the conception, common to all religions that reckon with real evil, of 'a spiritual power of evil... overruled to work out the judgments of God.' The reference of divination to such spirits, he adds, appears in the New Testament also (Acts 16:16-18).

Yet the difference is one of precision, not of substance, and the witnesses close ranks on the point that matters. Whatever name is given the volunteer — Satan by Gill, a subordinate 'power of evil' by Ellicott — both insist on its absolute subordination. Ellicott stresses that the invariable subjection of such spirits throughout the Old Testament 'precludes all danger of the monstrous dualism of so many Eastern religions'; the lying spirit does nothing but by leave, and serves, against its own will, the purposes of the throne before which it stands. The reader is left with a doctrine as sobering as it is safe: evil is real and personal, but it is never sovereign; even the father of lies can execute no deception except the one heaven has appointed for a judgment already deserved.
22:
The spirit's proposal — 'I will go forth, and I will be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets' — and the Lord's answer, 'Thou shalt persuade him, and prevail also: go forth, and do so,' bring the witnesses to the hardest question of the chapter: how does a God who cannot lie authorize a lie? The reformed John Gill handles the Lord's preliminary 'Wherewith?' by denying it implies ignorance: the question is asked 'not as ignorant of the scheme of the evil spirit, but in order to bring it out,' to lead on the narrative and lay the method bare for the reader's instruction.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke presses the language of permission that will govern the next verse too. The Lord's 'Go forth, and do so' means no more than this: 'God has permitted the spirit of lying to influence the whole of thy prophets, and he now, by my mouth, apprises thee of this, that thou mayest not go and fall at Ramoth-gilead.' Clarke turns the whole vision into an act of mercy laid bare. 'Never was a man more circumstantially and fairly warned'; Ahab 'had counsels from the God of truth, and counsels from the spirit of falsity; he obstinately forsook the former and followed the latter.' Even now, Clarke insists, it was still possible for him to make God his friend — 'but he would not: his blood was therefore upon his own head.'

The witnesses do not divide, and their shared instinct is to guard the holiness of God while affirming his sovereignty over the lie. God is not the author of the deception, but he governs it; he permits the lying spirit to do what Ahab's own hardened heart desires, and then, astonishingly, warns Ahab plainly of the very trap he is permitting — so that the king walks into his ruin over the wreckage of a clear revelation. The vision is therefore no excuse for Ahab and no stain on God. It shows a man given up to the delusion he preferred, and a mercy that pursued him even into the hall of judgment, laying the whole machinery of his deception open before his eyes, that he might yet turn and live.
23:
Micaiah drives the vision home — 'the LORD hath put a lying spirit in the mouth of all these thy prophets, and the LORD hath spoken evil concerning thee' — and the witnesses labour, with one accord, to hold together God's sovereignty and God's holiness. The reformed John Gill parses the strong verb carefully: the Lord 'suffered the lying spirit to suggest a lie' to the prophets, and sent them strong delusions to believe it, working through minds already disposed to flatter Ahab, so as to bring him to the ruin appointed for him. God's putting is a permitting that governs, not a creating of the falsehood.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke states the hermeneutical rule the verse requires. 'He hath permitted or suffered a lying spirit to influence thy prophets.' And Clarke draws out the principle that unlocks a hundred such passages: the Scriptures 'repeatedly represent God as doing what, in the course of his providence, he only permits or suffers to be done,' for 'nothing can be done in heaven, in earth, or hell, but either by his immediate energy or permission.' That is why the text can say God put the lie there without making God the liar. Charles Ellicott adds the note of vindication: the emphatic double naming of Jehovah here is an implied answer to Ahab's insinuation of mere personal malice in v.8 and v.18 — this is no grudge of Micaiah's, but the LORD's own word.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the doctrine and its warning. The whole matter is 'represented after the manner of men'; we are not to imagine that God is ever the author of sin or the cause of any man's telling or believing a lie. Rather, for the punishment of those who 'receive not the truth in the love of it,' God gives men up to strong delusions to believe a lie (compare 2 Thessalonians 2:11; Revelation 20:7-8), and even the deceiver serves his purposes (Job 12:16). Those, Henry concludes, are 'manifestly marked for ruin' who are thus given up to be imposed upon by lying prophets. The witnesses are united: God has spoken evil concerning Ahab, yet the lie is Ahab's chosen poison, and heaven's only hand in it is the just withdrawal that leaves a hardened man to the flatterers he loves.
24:
Zedekiah's answer to the vision is a blow — he 'smote Micaiah on the cheek' and sneered, 'Which way went the Spirit of the LORD from me to speak unto thee?' — and the witnesses read the violence as the false prophet's confession of defeat. The reformed John Gill notes the passion in the movement: he 'stepped in haste' from his place. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke hears in the taunt an insolence matching the assault: 'Did the Spirit of the Lord, who rests solely upon me, condescend to inspire thee? Was it at this ear' — where he struck him — 'that it entered, in order to hold communion with thee?' The blow and the boast are one gesture, a claim to monopolize the Spirit whose word has just exposed him.

Charles Ellicott weighs the act's full meaning. The smiting on the cheek is not merely contempt (Isaiah 1:6; Micah 5:1; Matthew 5:39) but 'professed indignation at words of blasphemy against God' — the very pretext under which the same insult would later be offered to the Lord himself and to Paul (John 18:22-23; Acts 23:2). The taunt sarcastically mocks Micaiah's claim to know the secret dealings of God, and Micaiah's reply, Ellicott notes, does not stoop to it but simply foretells the shame in which Zedekiah will one day discover who spoke the truth.

The Puritan Matthew Henry sets the scene in a longer light. The false prophets, he says, were always the worst enemies the true prophets had, not only stirring up the government against them but abusing them with their own hands; and he sees in the smitten cheek of Micaiah a foreshadowing of the Judge of Israel himself, buffeted and struck (compare Matthew 26:67; Micah 5:1). Yet Micaiah does not return the blow — 'God's prophets are not strikers nor persecutors,' and dare not avenge themselves — but leaves Zedekiah to be convinced by the event. The witnesses agree: the man who has run out of truth reaches for his fist, and the meekness that absorbs the blow is itself a kind of vindication, the quiet strength of one who can afford to wait for God to answer.
25:
Micaiah's calm reply to the blow — 'Behold, thou shalt see in that day, when thou shalt go into an inner chamber to hide thyself' — the witnesses read as a prophecy of the very terror Zedekiah is now bravely denying. The reformed John Gill canvasses the likely scene: Zedekiah would either accompany Ahab to the battle and, on the defeat, flee to the first hiding-place for safety, or, when news of the disaster reached Samaria, betake himself to a private chamber for fear of the enemy — or even for fear of the populace, who would lay the king's death at the door of the prophets who had urged the war.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads it as pointing to some divine judgment that overtook the false prophet. Hearing of the tragic outcome of the battle, Zedekiah 'no doubt went into a secret place to hide himself from the resentment of Jezebel, and the Israelitish courtiers, and there it is probable he perished' — though how, when, or where, the text does not say. The horns of iron that had promised triumph would end by driving their maker into a corner to await his own ruin.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the maxim the scene was made to teach: 'Those that will not have their mistakes rectified in time by the word of God will be undeceived, when it is too late, by the judgments of God.' Zedekiah, boasting most of the Spirit at the moment he knew least of his operations, is left to learn the truth not from a sermon but from a catastrophe. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the two prophets side by side — the one struck and serene, sure of a vindication he need not seize; the other loud and violent, hurrying toward the day when his confidence will collapse into a hiding-place. The verse is the whole contest in miniature: truth can wait, because the event will prove it; the lie must shout, because nothing else will hold it up. Gill's and Clarke's guesses at Zedekiah's end converge on the same picture — a man who had brandished iron horns before two thrones reduced to cowering in a back room — and that reversal is exactly the vindication Micaiah is content to leave to God rather than to seize with his own hand.
26:
Ahab's order — 'Take Micaiah, and carry him back' to Amon the governor of the city and to Joash the king's son — the witnesses treat as a brief but telling glimpse of how power disposes of a truth it cannot answer. The reformed John Gill notes only that the command was given to some of the king's officers; the word 'back' confirms what the earlier verses implied, that Micaiah was returned to a confinement he had never really left. Charles Ellicott fills in the two custodians: Joash 'the king's son,' of whom nothing further is known, is apparently entrusted, like the seventy sons of Ahab in a later day (2 Kings 10:1), to the charge of the city's governor, and may in theory have been left in command of Samaria with him.

The verse is spare, and its meaning lies in what Ahab does not do. He has heard a vision of the heavenly court, a plain word that he will fall at Ramoth, and a warning that his four hundred prophets are the instruments of a lying spirit; and his whole response is an order to remove the man from his sight and lodge him in a cell. He will not argue the prophecy, for he cannot; he will not heed it, for he will not; so he silences the voice and proceeds. It is the reflex of every power that meets a truth it dislikes — not refutation, but detention.

The witnesses, few and agreed, let the administrative note carry its own weight. To 'carry him back' is to treat the word of God as an inconvenience to be filed away, and the reader who knows the outcome sees the futility of it: the prison can hold the prophet, but it cannot touch the prophecy. Ahab consigns Micaiah to Amon and Joash and rides out to Ramoth, imagining he has settled the matter, when in truth he has only sealed his own testimony against himself — for the arrangement he makes 'until I come in peace' is precisely the peace he will never see. Ellicott's small prosopography of the custodians underlines the ordinariness of the moment: a governor, a king's son, a cell — the everyday furniture of authority pressed into service to muffle a word that no lock can silence.
27:
The sentence Ahab pronounces — 'Put this fellow in the prison, and feed him with bread of affliction and with water of affliction, until I come in peace' — the witnesses read as a study in bravado stretched over dread. The reformed John Gill pictures the place: the common prison of the city, where Micaiah had been before, now perhaps a more confined cell, what the old writers called 'little ease.' The Wesleyan Adam Clarke glosses the diet: to feed him with bread and water of affliction is to 'deprive him of all the conveniences and comforts of life... just keep him alive, that he may see my triumph.'

Charles Ellicott hears in the very harshness a confession. The command is one of severe treatment as well as scanty fare (compare Isaiah 30:20), and Ahab's 'affectation of disbelief — which his subsequent conduct shows to be but affectation — simply draws down a plainer and sterner prediction.' The phrase 'until I come in peace' is the tell: a man truly certain of victory would not need to threaten the prophet who denied it. And Ellicott adds a sober guess about Micaiah's end — of his fate we know nothing, 'but it is hard to suppose that his bold and defiant testimony could escape the extreme penalty of death, when Ahab's fall gave opportunity of revival to the ruthlessness of Jezebel.'

The witnesses do not differ, and the verse becomes a portrait of unbelief overplaying its hand. Ahab stakes everything on the words 'until I come in peace,' pinning the prophet's fate to his own safe return, and in doing so he makes his cruelty a wager against the word of God. The reader already knows how the wager falls. The bread of affliction Ahab orders for Micaiah is, in the event, the last order of a doomed man; the prophet will eat it in peace, and the king will never come home to end the meal. So the sentence meant to demonstrate Ahab's confidence stands instead as the measure of his folly, a threat that time will convert into a testimony.
28:
Micaiah's parting words — 'If thou return at all in peace, the LORD hath not spoken by me. Hearken, O people, every one of you' — the witnesses receive as the prophet staking his whole office on the event. The reformed John Gill reads the wager plainly: Micaiah is 'content to be reckoned a false prophet, and to be punished as such,' if Ahab comes home alive. It is the boldest possible test, and he offers it without a tremor, because, as the Puritan Matthew Henry says, 'he knew whom he had believed'; he ran no hazard in the appeal, for God 'will not fail to confirm the word of his servants' (compare Isaiah 44:26), and would rather let a thousand kings fall to the ground than let one jot of his own word fail.

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the closing summons and correct a common softening of it. The word rendered 'people' is not the term for a single people but for the nations: Micaiah 'invokes not only the persons present as witnesses of the truth of his words, but the nations generally, Israel and the surrounding nations,' who were to discern the truth of his word from the events that would follow (compare the same appeal in Micah 1:2). The prophet calls the whole world to watch the outcome of a single battle.

Charles Ellicott notes the striking verbal coincidence and the textual question it raises: 'Hearken, O people' are the very opening words of the prophetic Book of Micah, and, being absent from some manuscripts of the Septuagint, are supposed by some to be an early interpolation drawn from that book into this passage. The witnesses do not divide over the substance. Whether the phrase is Micaiah's own or an echo lent him by a later hand, its force is the same: the true prophet, unlike the flatterers who whispered what a king wished to hear, is willing to submit his message to the plainest public test and to summon heaven and earth as its judges. He has nothing to hide and nothing to fear from the verdict of the facts, and with that appeal he passes off the stage, leaving the outcome to vindicate the word.
29:
The two kings go up to Ramoth-gilead in defiance of the vision, and the witnesses fasten on the harder puzzle — not why Ahab went, but why Jehoshaphat did. The reformed John Gill gathers the reasons that might have inclined the good king: the four hundred prophets were agreed and used the name of the Lord; Micaiah's evil word touched Ahab only, so Jehoshaphat might think himself safe; he had given his word; and Ahab's cause, the recovery of his own city, was just. Keil and Delitzsch judge more sternly that he was 'probably deterred by false shame' from retracting an unconditional promise merely because of a prophecy Ahab claimed sprang from personal dislike — and 'narrowly escaped paying the penalty for it with his life,' earning on his return the prophet Jehu's rebuke (2 Chronicles 19:2).

The Puritan Matthew Henry locates the fault in temperament. Jehoshaphat, 'that pious prince,' who had himself asked for a prophet of the Lord out of distaste for Ahab's, is now 'carried away with the delusion' of his friends by the mere easiness of his nature — as Barnabas was once carried away by dissimulation (Galatians 2:13). He gave too much heed to Ahab's prophets because they too pretended to speak from God, and, never having been imposed on by such cheats in his own realm, 'was ready to conclude that it was four hundred to one but they should succeed.' Good nature, undefended by resolution, becomes a door for other men's sins.

Charles Ellicott reads the continued adhesion politically: Jehoshaphat's subservient part throughout betrays 'a position of virtual dependence of Judah on the stronger power of Israel,' of which the marriage alliance was at once the sign and the cause — an alliance 'destined to be all but fatal to the dynasty of David' (2 Kings 11:1-2). The witnesses converge on a single warning drawn from a good man's misstep: piety does not cancel the consequences of a rash entanglement, and the same easy temper that made Jehoshaphat a delightful ally made him a defenceless one. He goes up to a battle his conscience had already questioned, carried by courtesy toward a field where he will very nearly die.
30:
Ahab's stratagem — 'I will disguise myself, and enter into the battle; but put thou on thy robes' — the witnesses read as the exquisite self-contradiction of a man who half-believes the prophecy he is trying to outwit. The reformed John Gill takes the disguise as a change from royal robes into a common soldier's dress, or, on Abarbanel's view, the donning of a coat of mail — in any case a device 'to elude the prophecy of Micaiah.' Keil and Delitzsch catch the anxiety in the very grammar, an infinitive absolute that betrays the fear driving Ahab to such trouble for his own safety, while 'put on thy clothes' is no command but a permission — Jehoshaphat may wear his royal dress, since there is no danger to him.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke presses a textual point with real bite. Ahab had probably heard of Ben-hadad's order to strike at the king of Israel alone, and disguised himself accordingly; but the Septuagint reads the second clause 'but put thou on my robes.' Did Jehoshaphat, then, actually put on Ahab's royal robes? Clarke thinks the sequel suggests it, for it was this that caused the Syrians to mistake him for the king of Israel in v.32 — a reading that turns Ahab's precaution into a piece of cold treachery toward his ally.

Charles Ellicott supplies the moral portrait. The precaution is 'almost ludicrously characteristic of his temper of half-belief and half-unbelief': it is plainly absurd to grant that God's judgment has in all probability been pronounced, and yet to imagine it can be averted 'by so puerile a precaution.' Absurd, Ellicott grants, yet 'not the less on that account true to human nature, especially such a nature as his, always halting between two opinions.' The witnesses do not divide; they hold up a man who believes the word enough to fear it and defies it enough to march, and who thinks a change of clothing can hide him from the God whose arrow, the next verses will show, finds its mark through every disguise. There is a cruelty in the scheme the witnesses do not miss: to send a friend into the field in royal robes, conspicuous as a target, while the true king slips into the ranks unmarked, is to buy one's own safety with another man's danger. The precaution that fails to save Ahab very nearly destroys Jehoshaphat, and the whole device stands as a monument to the folly of thinking cunning can outmanoeuvre the word of God.
31:
The Syrian king's order to his chariot-captains — 'Fight neither with small nor great, save only with the king of Israel' — the witnesses read as the noose Ahab had unwittingly knotted for himself. The reformed John Gill notes the thirty-two captains, the same number as the kings and captains of the earlier Syrian wars, now commanding the divisions of the host. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott mark the singular concentration of the command: Syria's power has fully recovered, and it is aimed 'with singular virulence against the person of the king' who had once unwisely spared it, so that Ahab, the mover of the whole war, becomes the one target of the field.

The Puritan Matthew Henry reads the order as retribution long deferred. Ahab is now 'justly repaid for sparing Ben-hadad, who, as the seed of the serpent commonly do, stung the bosom in which he was fostered and saved from perishing.' The clemency the prophet had once condemned (1 Kings 20:42) returns upon Ahab's own head: the man he had released with a treaty now hunts him with thirty-two captains. Some suppose Ben-hadad meant only to take him alive and return the honour he had once received; whatever the design, the whole force of Syria is turned upon the single life that heaven had marked.

The witnesses converge on the terrible economy of the scene. Ahab has laid every human plan — the alliance that swells his army, the disguise that hides his person, a friend dressed as a king to draw off attention — and against them all stands one order that reduces the entire battle to a manhunt for him alone. The reader sees the two designs, Syrian and divine, running along the same line: Ben-hadad wants the king of Israel, and so does the God whose word Ahab imprisoned. The disguise that was meant to defeat the prophecy has only made the coming arrow, when it strikes, the plainer proof that no armour and no cunning can shelter a man from a judgment he has earned. Henry's word about the serpent stung by the bosom that warmed it is the moral centre of the scene, for the whole tragedy of Ramoth is a lesson in the folly of a mercy misplaced: had Ahab dealt justly with Ben-hadad when the prophet bade him, there would have been no thirty-two captains hunting his life today. The kindness he showed his enemy returns as his enemy's kindness to him.
32:
The captains, seeing a robed king, mistake Jehoshaphat for Ahab — 'Surely it is the king of Israel' — and surround him, until his cry undeceives them. The reformed John Gill notes simply that they saw him in his royal robes and took him for the man they sought. Charles Ellicott reads the cry as a rally, an appeal to gather his own people about him, and points to the fuller word of the Chronicler: 'the Lord helped him; and God moved them to depart from him' (2 Chronicles 18:31). The deliverance was no accident of recognition but the hand of God turning the captains aside.

The Puritan Matthew Henry finds in the peril and the rescue two messages at once. By the danger, God let Jehoshaphat know his displeasure at the confederacy: the king who had said in compliment to Ahab, 'I am as thou art' (v.4), is now taken for him in earnest — for 'those that associate with evildoers are in danger of sharing in their plagues.' Yet by the rescue, God let him know that though displeased he had not deserted him. Some captains withdrew on recognizing him, but the deeper cause, as the Chronicler says, was that God moved them; and Jehoshaphat's cry, Henry insists, was 'not in cowardice, but devotion,' a prayer that brought its own relief while Ahab took no thought to help him.

The witnesses land together on the doubleness of the moment: a good man reaping the danger of a bad alliance and the mercy of a faithful God in the same instant. Jehoshaphat's compliment is answered literally — he wears, in the enemy's eyes, the very identity he had so warmly claimed — and the fright is the exact measure of his fault. But the God he cried to is 'a friend that will not fail us when other friends do,' and the same providence that let the arrow find Ahab drew the swords away from Jehoshaphat. The verse rebukes and comforts at once, teaching that the Lord may chasten his own without abandoning them, and that a cry from the middle of one's own folly is never beneath his notice.
33:
The captains break off — 'when the captains of the chariots perceived that it was not the king of Israel... they turned back from pursuing him' — and the witnesses read the reprieve as the exact fulfilment of the Syrian order and the exact mercy of God. The reformed John Gill notes that their commission bound them to fight against the king of Israel only, so that once they knew Jehoshaphat was not their man they had no charge against him and let him go. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the sequence from the Hebrew: seeing him in royal dress they had pressed upon him, but at his cry they perceived their mistake and turned away, the same verb of turning aside that the narrative has already used.

The verse is brief, but its irony is complete. The very command that made Ahab the sole target — 'save only with the king of Israel' — is what saves Jehoshaphat: the discipline of the Syrian order, meant for one king's destruction, becomes another king's deliverance the instant the captains learn their error. Human strategy, narrowly aimed, serves a purpose beyond itself; the arrow that will find Ahab is loosed in the same field where the swords are sheathed against Jehoshaphat, and both are governed by the same overruling hand.

The witnesses, few and agreed, let the moment turn on a hair. Had the captains not paused to look, or had Jehoshaphat not cried out, the good king might have fallen in the bad king's place; but the God who had marked Ahab for judgment had not marked Jehoshaphat, and so the pursuers themselves are made to draw the line that providence intended. It is a quiet demonstration of the truth the whole chapter presses — that the issues of battle, which look to the soldiers like the fortunes of recognition and mischance, are in fact the working out of a word already spoken. The captains turn back exactly where heaven meant them to turn. Gill's plain remark about their commission and K&D's note on the turning verb together frame the lesson without embellishment: the very obedience of the Syrians to an order aimed at Ahab is what preserves the king it was not aimed at, so that even the enemy's discipline is bent to serve the ends of providence. What spares Jehoshaphat is not his cleverness but the plain fact that God had not marked him for death, and the pursuers themselves are made to prove it.
34:
The arrow that undoes all Ahab's cunning — 'a certain man drew a bow at a venture, and smote the king of Israel between the joints of the harness' — draws from the witnesses their fullest meditation on providence hidden in chance. The reformed John Gill states the paradox exactly: the archer shot 'without any intention to smite any particular person,' not knowing where the king was, 'yet God directed the arrow to the man he had marked for destruction, and neither his disguise, nor coat of mail, could secure from that.' A Jewish tradition, Gill and Charles Ellicott note, names the archer Naaman, the Syrian captain (compare 2 Kings 5:1).

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke weighs the difficult word rendered 'at a venture' — lethummo, which may mean in his simplicity, his integrity, or his perfection, that is, to the utmost of his skill and strength, drawing the string back to the arrow's head. He illustrates the shot from the old ballad of Chevy Chase, where an English archer, seeing his lord slain, drew 'an arrow of a cloth-yard long up to the head' and set it so true that the grey goose-wing was wet in his enemy's heart's blood. As for the fatal gap, Clarke reports the guesses — between cuirass and helmet, or cuirass and thigh-pieces — and the Vulgate's rendering, 'between the lungs and the stomach,' in the region of the heart.

Charles Ellicott adds the antiquarian detail, that the war-chariot held but two, the warrior and his driver, and that this is the first place where the chariot Solomon had imported (1 Kings 10:29) is mentioned as actually used in war, behind the proverb of a later day, 'the chariot of Israel and the horsemen thereof' (2 Kings 2:12). But the theology belongs to the Puritan Matthew Henry: the random shaft hit 'the right person' and 'the right place,' the one joint in the armour through which death could enter, for 'no armour is of proof against the darts of divine vengeance.' That which to us seems altogether casual, Henry concludes, 'is done by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God.' The witnesses are one: the venture of the archer is the aim of heaven, and the arrow no man guided goes straight to the heart the word had condemned.
35:
Ahab's long, upright dying — 'the king was stayed up in his chariot against the Syrians, and died at even, and the blood ran out of the wound into the midst of the chariot' — the witnesses read as a strange dignity granted to a doomed man. Keil and Delitzsch render the scene closely: the conflict 'ascended,' grew more violent like a rising flood, while the king kept himself upright in the chariot before the Syrians, that he might not dishearten his soldiers, and poured out his life-blood into the chariot's hollow until evening. Adam Clarke notes the motive of the concealment: he did not wish his misfortune known, 'lest his troops should be discouraged.'

Charles Ellicott reads the death with a moralist's eye. Ahab's repentance, imperfect as it was, has at least availed to secure him 'a warrior's death, before the evil came on his house'; wounded and disabled, he conceals the deadliness of his hurt and bravely sustains the battle till his strength fails. Ellicott finds in it a familiar human paradox — 'this union of desperate physical bravery with moral feebleness and cowardice' — the very combination, he adds, that Shakespeare drew in Macbeth, most true to nature.

The Puritan Matthew Henry supplies the inner terror the outward courage hides. Ahab had himself carried from the press to have his wound dressed, yet insisted on being held up to watch the battle; and as he saw it turn against his army, 'his spirits sunk, and he died — but his death was so lingering that he had time to feel himself die.' With what horror, Henry imagines, he now reflected on the wickedness he had committed — Baal's altars, Naboth's vineyard, Micaiah's imprisonment — seeing himself flattered into his own ruin, and Zedekiah's horns of iron pushing not the Syrians but himself into destruction. The witnesses do not divide: they leave Ahab propped bleeding in his chariot at sunset, brave to the last and lost to the last, a man who spent his final hours watching the exact fulfilment of the vision he had jailed the prophet for speaking.
36:
As the sun goes down a proclamation runs through the host — 'Every man to his city, and every man to his own country' — and the witnesses read the cry as the quiet end of a war that had lost its cause with its author. The reformed John Gill sets the timing about the hour of Ahab's death, and supposes the proclamation made either by the dying king's order or by Jehoshaphat once he learned that Ahab was dead. Keil and Delitzsch render the moment plainly: toward sunset the cry went through the army drawn up for battle, 'Every one into his city and into his land.'

Adam Clarke supplies the reason the fighting simply stopped. The Israelites and Jews had maintained the battle the whole day, but when at evening the king died and it became known, a proclamation went out — 'probably with the consent of both Syrians and Israelites' — that the war was over. Ahab being dead, his subjects had no heart to contend further for Ramoth-gilead; so the Israelites went to their cities and the Syrians to their country, and the disputed fortress was left where it had lain.

The witnesses do not differ, and the verse quietly fulfils the first of Micaiah's visions. The prophet had seen 'all Israel scattered upon the hills, as sheep that have not a shepherd' (v.17), and here the shepherd is smitten and the flock disperses — not in slaughter but in the orderly retreat of a leaderless people sent home. The whole campaign, launched on a chorus of four hundred voices promising triumph, ends with a single herald's cry telling every man to go home. Nothing has been gained; the city Ahab coveted remains Syrian; and the only thing the war has accomplished is the death heaven foretold. The proclamation is the epitaph of a self-willed enterprise, and the reader hears in it the vindication of the prophet the king had chained. Clarke's observation that both armies consented to end the fighting only sharpens the pathos: no one much wanted Ramoth once the man who wanted it was dead, and the thousands mustered on a promise of glory melt away at a herald's word, each to his own city, leaving the field as empty as the boast that had filled it.
37:
The bare notice — 'So the king died, and was brought to Samaria; and they buried the king in Samaria' — the witnesses read as the deliberate hinge on which the whole prophecy turns. Keil and Delitzsch catch the compression of the Hebrew: 'thus the king died and came to Samaria' means, in effect, the king reached Samaria dead. He set out for Ramoth alive and defiant, arranging his prison and his provisions 'until I come in peace'; he returns a corpse. The clause is short because the point is stark: the king came home, but not as he had promised, and not in peace.

The verse fulfils to the letter Micaiah's public wager. 'If thou return at all in peace, the LORD hath not spoken by me' (v.28), the prophet had cried, calling the nations to witness; and now the dead king is carried through the gate where the two thrones had stood, past the place of his bravado, to a grave in his own capital. The people he had summoned to hear his sentence on Micaiah are the same who now bury him, the living proof that the imprisoned prophet had spoken and the four hundred had lied.

The witnesses, few and united, let the fact do its own work. There is no gloating in the narrative and none in the commentators; the sentence simply records that a man went out to overturn a word of God and came back to be buried under it. Every precaution Ahab had taken — the alliance, the disguise, the friend dressed as a king — is answered by this one line, and the reader is left to measure the distance between 'until I come in peace' and 'they buried the king in Samaria.' The word of the Lord, spoken by a prisoner and mocked by a court, stands; and the burial is its seal. K&D's terse gloss — that the king 'reached Samaria dead' — is the whole irony compressed into three words: he did come back to his capital, exactly as he had said he would, and yet not in the peace he had staked the prophet's life upon. The verse asks nothing of the reader but that he set the promise and the outcome side by side and draw the plain conclusion, which is that God's word does not return void, and that the grave of a king who defied it is its quietest and most conclusive vindication.
38:
The gruesome sequel — the chariot washed in the pool of Samaria, the dogs licking Ahab's blood — the witnesses read as the precise redemption of a prophetic threat, though they divide over the second half of the verse. The reformed John Gill pictures the chariot-driver plunging the blood-stained car into the pool to cleanse it.
