The KJV adds that 'they washed his armour,' but Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott follow the Septuagint (supported by Josephus) in reading instead that 'the harlots bathed' in the bloodied water — for the same Hebrew, hazzonoth rachatsu, can be rendered either way. Ellicott judges the KJV a mistranslation, and finds a grim fitness in the true sense: 'the dog and the harlot are the animal and human types of uncleanness,' met together at the pool where a defiled king's blood is washed away.

The point on which all agree is the fulfilment of the word. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott alike refer the licking of the blood to Elijah's sentence against Ahab (1 Kings 21:19) and behind it the unknown prophet's word (1 Kings 20:42) and Micaiah's own. Ellicott honestly registers the difficulty that a later notice places the requital in Naboth's field at Jezreel (2 Kings 9:25), and grants the reconcilement hard; yet he holds that the plain reference here, fixed by the context to Samaria, must outweigh it, Naboth having likely owned land in both places.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gathers the theology. 'Now Naboth's blood was avenged,' and both Elijah's word and David's were fulfilled — 'that thy foot may be dipped in the blood of thy enemies, and the tongue of thy dogs in the same' (Psalms 68:23). The dogs, Henry observes, 'made no difference between royal blood and other blood,' and he sees in the scene a figure of 'the terrors that prey upon the guilty soul after death.' The witnesses converge, past their textual dispute, on one sober lesson: the word of the Lord is exact to the letter and no throne exempts a man from it. Ahab's blood, licked by dogs in the pool where the unclean washed, is the receipt heaven writes across the treaty and the murder and the mocked prophecy alike.
39:
The formulaic close of Ahab's reign lingers on one detail — 'the ivory house which he made' — and the witnesses read the luxury as a silent commentary on the man. The reformed John Gill supposes it not built wholly of ivory but richly inlaid with it, and recalls the 'ivory houses' of Scripture (compare Psalms 45:8). Keil and Delitzsch and Adam Clarke agree it was a palace inlaid with ivory in his capital, against which the prophet Amos would later thunder (Amos 3:15).

Charles Ellicott presses the historical resonance. Here, 'for the first time since the days of Solomon' (1 Kings 10:18-20), the use of ivory — so characteristic of Zidonian art — reappears, an 'undesigned coincidence' in perfect keeping with Ahab's renewed intercourse with Zidon through his marriage to Jezebel. The 'cities that he built' were possibly those ceded by Ben-hadad and rebuilt as strongholds. The whole notice, Ellicott observes, shows that Ahab's reign was 'externally one of power and prosperity, as yet unimpaired even by his death and disaster at Ramoth-gilead' — 'the fruits of spiritual corruption had not yet ripened.'

The witnesses do not contend, and the ivory house becomes a parable of the reign it adorned. Ahab leaves behind him a monument of splendour and a memory of judgment: a palace panelled in imported luxury, and a body just carried home from a battle he had been warned not to fight. Gill's and Clarke's glance toward Amos is the point — the prophet who would denounce the ivory houses of Israel saw in them the emblem of a wealth divorced from righteousness, a magnificence that God would smite. So the historian, closing the account, sets the builder's finest work beside his bloody end, and lets the reader feel how little the ivory could purchase for a king who had sold himself to do evil. Prosperity is not the measure of a man before God, and the gleaming house outlives its owner only to accuse him. Ellicott's phrase — that the fruits of spiritual corruption 'had not yet ripened' — is the sober key to the whole notice, for the splendour was real and the collapse still to come, and it is precisely such a season of unpunished magnificence that most tempts a nation to mistake the patience of God for his approval.
40:
The reign ends in the settled formula — 'So Ahab slept with his fathers; and Ahaziah his son reigned in his stead' — and the witnesses hear in its very ordinariness a note of terrible mercy and terrible continuity. The reformed John Gill observes only that more of Ahaziah is to be said in the latter part of the chapter and the book to follow. Keil and Delitzsch note that the phrase 'slept with his fathers' is used even of Ahab, whose end was anything but peaceful, and that the historian now turns from the fallen king of Israel to open the summary of Jehoshaphat's reign in Judah.

The gentle idiom repays attention. 'Slept with his fathers' is the language of a quiet decease, and it is laid over a man shot through with an arrow and licked by dogs — a reminder that Scripture's set phrases do not flatter, but simply record the passing of a generation. Ahab, for all his violence and his violent end, dies in his bed of ancestors as kings do, and his throne passes without a tremor to his son. The dynasty Jezebel had shaped continues; the Baal-worship is not buried with its patron.

The witnesses, few and agreed, let the succession sound its warning. That Ahaziah reigns 'in his stead' is the sombre thread the chapter's last verses will pull: the son inherits not only the crown but the sin, and the reader who has watched the father flatter himself into destruction is prepared to watch the son do the same. The death of one hardened king is no purging of the house; the arrow that felled Ahab did not touch the idolatry he leaves behind. So the formula that closes a reign quietly opens a judgment yet to run its course, and the peace of 'slept with his fathers' is only the pause before the same rebellion, in a new hand, provokes the same God. Gill's forward glance to 'the latter part of this chapter and the following book' is the historian's own hint that the story is not finished with Ahab's grave: the crown changes heads, but the house of Ahab remains the house of Ahab, and the reader who has learned from the father what defiance of the word costs is set, with a kind of dread, to watch the son begin the lesson again.
41:
With Ahab buried, the historian turns south to Judah, and the witnesses read the abrupt shift as a deliberate feature of the book's design. Charles Ellicott explains it: the narrative, so far as it is full and continuous, 'centres round the prophetic work of Elijah and Elisha,' whose scene was in Israel, so that the compiler 'contents himself with the insertion of a few brief annalistic notices of the kingdom of Judah,' taking up the thread dropped at 1 Kings 15:24. For the full and glowing record of Jehoshaphat's reign — his great religious revival above all — one must go to the Chronicler (2 Chronicles 17-20). The reformed John Gill fits the dates together: Jehoshaphat, beginning in Ahab's fourth year, reigned some eighteen years alongside Ahab and seven years after him.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the reign its measure, and it is one of praise shadowed by a single fault. Jehoshaphat's was 'none of the longest, but one of the best'; twenty-five years, yet in the prime of his life, and 'added to his father's happy forty-one' they yield a grateful picture of Judah flourishing in religion and peace while Israel decayed. Its one blemish, Henry insists, was the intimacy with the house of Ahab, which 'upon several accounts was a diminution to him' — the confederacy in war we have already seen dangerous, and the confederacy in trade will prove no better.

The witnesses do not divide; together they hold up a good king whose goodness was real and whose one weakness was a temptation to which good men are peculiarly liable — the desire to be at peace with everyone, even with those at war with God. Henry's balance is the lesson: it is better, he says, for a man long in reputation for wisdom and honour 'to die in the midst of it than to outlive it,' and Jehoshaphat, whatever his snares, kept his integrity to the end. The chapter that has shown the ruin of a hardened king now sets beside it the honour of a faithful one, and the contrast is the whole moral of the two kingdoms: the throne that fears God stands, while the throne that flatters itself falls.
42:
The formal register of Jehoshaphat's accession — 'thirty and five years old when he began to reign; and he reigned twenty and five years in Jerusalem... his mother's name was Azubah' — the witnesses treat as the plain scaffolding on which the reign's character is hung. The reformed John Gill does the arithmetic: five-and-thirty at his accession and five-and-twenty years on the throne make him sixty at his death, a full and honoured span. Keil and Delitzsch note that the same figures stand, with slight deviation, in the closing summary of the Chronicler (2 Chronicles 20:31-33), and press straight on to the verdict the next verse will render — that he walked wholly in the way of his father Asa.

The detail of the mother's name is not idle in the pattern of Kings, where the queen-mother is regularly recorded, for good or ill, as a mark of the influence that shaped a reign. Where the northern kingdom had a Jezebel behind its throne, feeding a table of Baal's prophets and steeling her husband and sons in idolatry, Judah had an Azubah whose son turned not aside from the ways of a godly father. The bare genealogical notice, set against the chapter's earlier scenes, quietly underlines how much the piety or apostasy of a house may hang upon the women who govern its nursery.

The witnesses, few and agreed here, let the register do its sober work. A king's years, his age, his mother, his city — these are the coordinates by which the sacred historian fixes a life before he weighs it, and in Jehoshaphat's case every coordinate is favourable. He comes to the throne in mature manhood, reigns a quarter-century in the city of David, and stands in a line of faith that reaches back through Asa. The verse asks no interpretation, only attention; but set beside the death of Ahab it makes its silent point, that a reign begun and continued in the fear of God is the thing worth recording, and that length of days in Jerusalem is the reward the narrative has been contrasting all along with the arrow at Ramoth.
43:
The verdict on Jehoshaphat — 'he walked in all the ways of Asa his father... nevertheless the high places were not taken away' — hands the witnesses their one exegetical knot in the closing section: how to square this 'nevertheless' with the Chronicler's report that he did take the high places away. The reformed John Gill simply registers the praise and its qualification, that the king who did what was right yet left this old corruption standing. Keil and Delitzsch read the commendation at full strength — he 'walked entirely in the way of his father Asa and departed not from it' — whereas Asa's own heart had cooled in his last years.

The tension is with 2 Chronicles 17:6, which says he 'took away the high places,' and the Wesleyan and grammatical-historical voices offer two ways to reconcile it. Adam Clarke proposes a distinction of kinds: there were high places used for idolatry and high places consecrated to the Lord before the Temple was built; the former Jehoshaphat removed, the latter he did not. Failing that, Clarke reports the conjecture that the Chronicler's text should read 'he did not take away,' a single letter's difference in the Hebrew. Charles Ellicott favours the first solution, sharpened: noting that the Chronicler couples 'the high places and the groves' (2 Chronicles 17:6), he takes the key to lie in the word Asherah — the base idol-shrines Jehoshaphat swept away, while the merely unauthorized sanctuaries of Jehovah worship remained, at least in part (compare 2 Chronicles 20:33).

The debate is friendly and its resolution agreed: idolatrous high places removed, irregular ones tolerated. The witnesses land on the pastoral point the 'nevertheless' was written to make — that even a good and reforming king may leave one field unweeded, one old and popular corruption unchallenged. The high places were near enough to Jerusalem that the people had no excuse of distance for offering there rather than at the altar; yet, Gill and Ellicott alike imply, old habits patronized by former good men are the hardest to root out. Jehoshaphat's blemish is not scandalous sin but incomplete obedience, and the record of it is a warning that a reign may be reckoned 'right in the eyes of the LORD' and still fall short of the thoroughness that God's honour deserves.
44:
The notice that 'Jehoshaphat made peace with the king of Israel' the witnesses read as at once his wisdom and his snare. The reformed John Gill takes it broadly — peace first with Ahab, sealed by the affinity and the joint expedition to Ramoth, and then with his successor Ahaziah. Charles Ellicott observes that the verse is 'chronologically out of place,' a summary reference to the policy Jehoshaphat pursued from the beginning: exchanging the chronic hostility that had marked the previous reigns for peace and alliance with the northern kingdom.

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the policy with a careful hand, granting the good and marking the excess. Jehoshaphat 'maintained peace with the king of Israel' — with every one of the Israelite kings of his time, Ahab, Ahaziah, and Joram — 'whereas hitherto the two kingdoms had assumed an attitude of hostility toward each other.' In itself, K&D allow, this bearing was 'laudable'; the fault lay in how far he carried it, for he 'went beyond the bounds of what was allowable' by forming a marriage alliance with the house of Ahab, giving his son Joram to a daughter of Ahab and Jezebel (2 Chronicles 18:1).

The witnesses converge on a distinction the reign itself was written to teach: peace is good, but not every means of peace is lawful. To end a needless feud between brother-kingdoms was a mercy; to cement it with a marriage that carried Jezebel's blood into the line of David was a folly all but fatal to that line. The commentators leave the two halves of the policy side by side — the honourable peace and the ruinous affinity — and let the reader see how easily a virtue overreaches into a fault. Jehoshaphat's readiness to be reconciled was the finest and the most dangerous thing about him, and this quiet verse, tucked among the annals, marks the exact point where a good man's generosity of temper began to mortgage his descendants' faith. K&D's careful weighing — laudable in itself, yet carried beyond bounds — is the pattern the witnesses commend for judging every such alliance: not to condemn peace-making as though enmity were holier, but to ask whether the bond entered has bought concord at the price of compromise. Jehoshaphat crossed that line at the marriage-bed, and the daughter of Ahab and Jezebel would one day nearly extinguish the house of David because of it.
45:
The closing formula — 'the rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, and his might that he shewed, and how he warred' — the witnesses use to gesture toward the fuller record the annals of Kings only summarize. The reformed John Gill notes that many of these acts are preserved in the canonical book of Chronicles, and that more once stood in the royal annals here referred to, out of which the Spirit transmitted what he saw fit for later ages. The verse is the historian's way of saying that a reign larger than his page is written elsewhere.

Keil and Delitzsch fill in what the 'might' and the 'wars' comprehend. The brave deeds Jehoshaphat performed include both his labours to strengthen the kingdom — raising fortifications, organizing the army, and, above all, instructing his people in the law and improving the administration of justice (2 Chronicles 17:7-19; 2 Chronicles 19:4-11) — and the wars he waged, the campaigns already told. The 'might' of this king, K&D quietly note, was as much moral and religious as military: he was strong because he taught his people the word of God and set justice on a surer footing.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse yields its own understated praise. Where the chapter's earlier scenes had shown a king of Israel surrounded by four hundred lying prophets, here is a king of Judah who sent teachers of the law through his cities and reformed his courts of justice. The contrast is the whole point of the two records set side by side: Ahab's strength was the ivory house and the swelling army that came to nothing at Ramoth, while Jehoshaphat's was the settled peace, the instructed people, and the honoured justice that made Judah flourish. The 'rest of his acts' the reader is sent to find in Chronicles are, in sum, the acts of a man whose might was rooted in reverence, and whose wars were the least of the reasons his reign is remembered. Gill's reminder that the inspired record preserves only a selection from a larger archive is itself a lesson in how Scripture writes history: it keeps not what magnifies a king's vanity but what serves the knowledge of God, so that a reign is measured in the end by its righteousness and not by the bulk of its annals. The teacher of the law and the reformer of the courts is the Jehoshaphat the Spirit chose to hand down.
46:
The note that Jehoshaphat 'took out of the land' the remnant of the sodomites left from Asa's day the witnesses read as the measure of a reforming zeal that finished what a good father had begun. The reformed John Gill draws the comparison: Asa had removed many of 'these filthy creatures,' no doubt as many as came to his knowledge, but some remained; and the son, of the same holy disposition, removed the rest. The reform ran in the family, and the son's honour was to complete it.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke presses the Hebrew and widens the sense. The word is haqqedeshim, the 'consecrated persons' — but consecrated to abomination, the male devotees of a polluted cult; and Clarke takes it to reach beyond the persons to 'the system of pollution, effeminacy, and debauch' itself, 'the abominations of Priapus, and the rites of Venus, Baal, and Ashtaroth.' What Jehoshaphat destroyed was not merely a class of offenders but a whole apparatus of ritual filth, so that 'no more of that impure worship was to be found in Judea' (compare 1 Kings 14:24; 1 Kings 15:12).

The witnesses stand together, and the verse balances the qualified verdict just before it. If the high places 'were not taken away,' this at least was: the cult prostitution that had crept in with Canaanite religion was swept out root and branch. The reformer who left one field unweeded cleared another entirely, and the two notices together give a true and unflattering portrait of even the best of Judah's kings — thorough where his zeal was awake, incomplete where old custom had lulled it. The lesson the commentators leave is that reformation is real though partial, and that a king is to be honoured for the corruption he does purge even while he is measured by the corruption he tolerates. Jehoshaphat's cleansing of the land of its grossest impurity is set to his lasting credit. Clarke's insistence that the reform reached the whole polluted system, not merely its individual practitioners, is the point worth carrying away: true reformation strikes at the roots of an evil and not only at its visible fruits, so that the thing itself is banished and cannot quietly regrow. Where Jehoshaphat's hand fell, it fell to the root; that it did not fall everywhere is the pity, but where it fell the land was clean.
47:
The parenthesis that 'there was then no king in Edom: a deputy was king' the witnesses read as a historian's careful footnote, laid down to prepare the reader for the next verse. The reformed John Gill explains the arrangement: Edom had been subdued since David's day, garrisoned and governed by deputies under the crown of Judah, and so it remained through Jehoshaphat's reign, until under his son the Edomites revolted and set up a king of their own; the note is added to show how Jehoshaphat could build ships at Ezion-geber, a port in Edomite territory then under his viceroy.

Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott add the historical frame. Clarke observes that the writer of the book plainly 'lived after the days of Jehoshaphat,' looking back on a settled fact: David had conquered Edom, and it was ruled by deputies of Judah's kings 'till they recovered their liberty' in the days of Jehoram (compare 2 Kings 8:22). Ellicott ties it firmly to the verses that follow — Ezion-geber being a seaport of Edom — and identifies the shadowy 'king of Edom' of a later campaign (2 Kings 3), a subject ally not consulted as an equal, with 'the deputy' of this passage; full Edomite independence, he notes, came only under Jehoram (2 Chronicles 21:8-10).

The witnesses do not divide, and the small clause repays the attention they give it. It is a reminder that the sacred history is knit together across its books: a passing remark about Edom's government exists only to make sense of a fleet, and the fleet in turn will illustrate the one snare that dogged Jehoshaphat's piety. The commentators' patience with so dry a notice models a discipline worth learning — that in Scripture even the driest administrative aside is placed with purpose, and that to read it in its connection is to watch the historian quietly lay the ground on which his next lesson will stand. Edom's deputy is here only that Jehoshaphat's ships may sail, and his ships are told of only that his one recurring folly may be exposed.
48:
Jehoshaphat's ill-fated fleet — 'ships of Tarshish to go to Ophir for gold: but they went not; for the ships were broken at Ezion-geber' — the witnesses read against the fuller account of the Chronicler, and they agree the wreck was a judgment. The reformed John Gill glosses 'ships of Tarshish' as sea-going vessels, ten in number by one reading. Adam Clarke notes the parallel that Jehoshaphat 'joined himself to Ahaziah' to build them (2 Chronicles 20:36), and that 'ships of Tarshish' may mean simply ships of burden. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott supply the decisive detail the annals here omit: the prophet Eliezer had denounced the alliance and foretold the destruction of the ships (2 Chronicles 20:37), and a storm broke the fleet in the harbour of Ezion-geber.

Ellicott sets the venture in its wider history. This 'revival of maritime enterprise' coincides pointedly with the renewed alliance, through Israel, with Tyre; the fleet was a combined enterprise of Judah and Israel, built at the Edomite port that belonged to Judah, and it was precisely the partnership with the house of Ahab that drew the prophetic rebuke and the storm. The gold of Ophir that Solomon's navy had once brought home (compare 1 Kings 10:22) will not come to a fleet yoked to an idolater.

The witnesses converge on the lesson the shipwreck was sent to teach. Jehoshaphat's fault was never gross impiety; it was the recurring readiness to be unequally yoked — first in war, now in trade — with a house at enmity with God. And God met the fault not with the loss of the king's soul but with the loss of his ships, a mercy in the form of a rod. The broken hulls at Ezion-geber preach what the arrow at Ramoth preached to Ahab, but in a gentler key: that alliances the Lord has forbidden will not prosper, however golden their promise, and that a friend of God who leans on the friendship of God's enemies must be taught, by the wreck of his fairest schemes, to lean no longer.
49:
The sequel to the shipwreck — Ahaziah's offer, 'Let my servants go with thy servants in the ships,' and the flat refusal, 'But Jehoshaphat would not' — the witnesses read as the moment a chastened king at last learns his lesson, though one voice questions the very text. Keil and Delitzsch reconstruct the sequence with the Chronicler's help: after the first ships were wrecked, Ahaziah pressed for a fresh attempt with a combined crew, but Jehoshaphat, having seen Eliezer's prediction come true, 'would not' venture again, and so the two accounts fall neatly into place. The Puritan Matthew Henry makes this refusal the heart of the story: 'the rod of God, expounded by the word of God, had effectually broken him off from his confederacy with that ungodly unhappy prince.'

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke stands apart, raising a textual doubt. Since 2 Chronicles 20:36 says Jehoshaphat did join Ahaziah in building ships, Clarke wonders whether this refusal belongs to a second, later proposal — or whether, following Houbigant, the Hebrew should read not 'he would not' but 'he consented to him,' the two differing by a single letter. On that reading the verses would be rearranged so that the consent came first and the wreck after. Clarke commends the conjecture as clearing a real difficulty, though he admits it rests on no manuscript.

Against Clarke's emendation the other witnesses hold the received text, and with it the finer moral. Henry draws it out: 'Better buy wisdom dear than be without it,' yet experience is called 'the mistress of fools' because only fools must be taught by it what the word of God had already told them. Jehoshaphat had needed a shipwreck to cure him of a friendship the prophets had warned against from the first; but, to his lasting credit, he was cured, and when the temptation came again he had grace to say no. The witnesses land on a hopeful note rare in this chapter of hardened refusals: here at last is a man who felt the rod, understood it, and changed — the difference, in the end, between a Jehoshaphat and an Ahab.
50:
The quiet close of Jehoshaphat's life — 'and Jehoshaphat slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David his father' — the witnesses read as the honourable rest of a faithful king. The reformed John Gill names the resting-place: the city of Zion, where David, Rehoboam, Abijam, and Asa were buried before him, so that Jehoshaphat is gathered into the sepulchre of the godly line from which he sprang. The bare notice sets him, in death as in life, among the kings who kept faith.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the death its dignity. His departure is spoken of here 'to shut up his story,' Henry notes, though the history of the northern kingdom will mention him once more afterward (compare 2 Kings 3:7). And Henry draws the reflection that Jehoshaphat's example prompts: it is better for a man long in reputation for wisdom and honour 'to die in the midst of it than to outlive it.' Unlike his father Asa, whose last years were clouded, Jehoshaphat carried his integrity unbroken to the grave and passed while his honour was still bright.

The witnesses, few and agreed, let the burial preach by contrast. Two kings die in this chapter, and the difference between their deaths is the difference between the two ways set before every reader. Ahab is carried home with an arrow-wound, his blood licked by dogs in the pool of Samaria, the exact fulfilment of a prophet's threat; Jehoshaphat sleeps with his fathers in the city of David, laid among the faithful, his son succeeding him in peace. The one grave is a judgment, the other a benediction. The chapter that opened on a war council closes on a tomb, and in that tomb it quietly commends the fear of God: the man who walked in the ways of Asa, whatever his snares, is buried in honour, and the last word over him is rest. Henry's observation that Jehoshaphat is heard of once more in the chronicles of Israel, after his story here is closed, is a final gentle irony — even in death the alliance with the north pursues him — but it cannot disturb the peace of the sepulchre in the city of David, where he lies gathered to a line of kings who, for all their faults, held the covenant. The reformed and puritan voices agree: to sleep with such fathers, in such a city, is the burial of a man whom God had owned.
51:
The book turns at last to Ahab's heir — 'Ahaziah the son of Ahab began to reign over Israel in Samaria the seventeenth year of Jehoshaphat... and reigned two years' — and the witnesses fasten on both the chronological knot and the character it introduces. The reformed John Gill works the arithmetic, granting the difficulty that Jehoshaphat began in Ahab's fourth year and Ahab reigned twenty-two, which would place Ahaziah's accession in Jehoshaphat's eighteenth; he resolves it by supposing Ahaziah made king in his father's lifetime, before the expedition to Ramoth, or that Ahab's twenty-two years were not complete. Charles Ellicott reads the reign's inner meaning: in this short span 'the influence of Jezebel, evidently in abeyance in the last days of Ahab, revives,' and the worship of Baal resumes its place beside the older idolatry of Jeroboam, and beside the Canaanite Baalzebub (2 Kings 1:2).

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the reign its moral weight. It was 'very short, not two years' — for 'some sinners God makes quick work with' — and a very bad character is fixed to it. Ahaziah had heard of the ruin of Jeroboam's family and had seen his own father drawn to destruction by the prophets of Baal, who had so often been proved false; yet he took no warning, but followed 'the example of his wicked father, and the counsel of his more wicked mother Jezebel, who was still living.'

The witnesses converge on the tragedy of inherited sin. 'Miserable are the children,' Henry laments, 'who not only derive a sinful nature from their parents, but are taught by them to increase it; and most unhappy parents are they, that help to damn their children's souls.' The chapter that showed a father flatter himself into ruin now shows a son embrace the same ruin with open eyes, unmoved by every warning his own household had furnished. 'Hardened sinners rush forward, unawed and unmoved, in the ways from which others before them have been driven into everlasting misery.' Jezebel's continuing presence behind the throne is the sombre thread: the queen who steeled Ahab now steels Ahaziah, and the reader sees how a house given to Baal reproduces itself, generation after generation, until judgment runs its full course.
52:
The formal indictment of Ahaziah — 'he did evil in the sight of the LORD, and walked in the way of his father, and in the way of his mother, and in the way of Jeroboam' — the witnesses read as the summing-up of a threefold apostasy. The reformed John Gill names the evil plainly: it was idolatry. Keil and Delitzsch unfold the three ways: Ahaziah walked in the way of his father and mother, 'who had introduced the worship of Baal into the kingdom,' and in the way of Jeroboam, 'who had set up the calves' (compare 1 Kings 16:30-33) — so that he gathered into himself both the old sin of the northern schism and the newer Baal-cult of his own dynasty.

Keil and Delitzsch pause over the placing of these verses and register a mild complaint against the received division of the text. With this general description of Ahaziah's character 'not only is the chapter brought to a close, but the first book of Kings also — very unsuitably, however,' since the further account of his reign and death runs on into the next book (2 Kings 1). It would have been far more fitting, K&D judge, to begin a fresh chapter, and indeed the second book itself, at this point, where a new reign is only just opening.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse leaves the reader on the threshold of a story deliberately unfinished. The chapter that began with a king consulting four hundred false prophets ends with a king who needed no prophet to lead him astray, for he walked knowingly in every well-trodden path of his house's rebellion. The three ways named — father, mother, Jeroboam — are the whole genealogy of Israel's ruin gathered into one young man, and the historian's refusal to say more than 'he did evil' is itself an ominous economy. The idolatry that felled Ahab is not buried with him; it walks on in his son, and the book of Kings closes not on a resolution but on a continuing rebellion whose reckoning the next book will record.
53:
The last verse of the book — 'For he served Baal, and worshipped him, and provoked to anger the LORD God of Israel, according to all that his father had done' — the witnesses read as the deliberate, damning summary on which the whole first book of Kings comes to rest. The reformed John Gill notes only the plain fact: Ahaziah served Baal 'as his father had done, and his mother still did.' Keil and Delitzsch repeat the sentence as the sealing of Ahaziah's character, adding that it is expressly noted, as of Ahab before him (compare 1 Kings 16:31), that he adored and worshipped Baal.

The verse's force lies in its closing phrase — 'according to all that his father had done.' The son is measured against the father, and found to be his exact image: the same Baal, the same provocation, the same anger of the God of Israel kindled afresh. Where a reader might hope that the arrow at Ramoth and the dogs at the pool had taught the house of Ahab something, this final line dashes the hope. The judgment that fell on the father is being freshly deserved by the son, who has learned from Ahab's ruin nothing but the way to repeat it.

The witnesses, few and united, let the book end on its gravest note. The narrative that opened long before with the glory of Solomon's Temple closes here on a king serving Baal and provoking the Lord — a measure of how far the kingdom has fallen and how patiently, and how justly, God's anger has been provoked. Yet the very phrase 'provoked to anger the LORD God of Israel' carries, beneath its severity, the covenant name: he is still 'the LORD God of Israel,' the God of a people he has not abandoned even in their apostasy. The book ends unresolved, its last king an idolater, its last word a provocation; but the God who is provoked is the God who keeps covenant, and the reader is left waiting, across the seam into the next book, for the judgment and the mercy that the God of Israel will yet unfold.
